text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025

children.text: Overall orientation
Mandates and background

9.1	The Department of Economic and Social Affairs supports the development pillar of the United Nations Secretariat by fostering international cooperation for sustainable development for all. The Department’s responsibilities include: (a) providing substantive support on development issues to the General Assembly, the Economic and Social Council and its functional commissions, and expert bodies; (b) monitoring and analysing development trends, prospects and policy issues globally; and (c) supporting the translation of policy frameworks developed in United Nations conferences and summits into policies and programmes at the country level and assisting Member States in building their national capacities. In doing so, the Department supports the implementation of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development and the achievement of its Sustainable Development Goals through, among others, the regular programme of technical cooperation and United Nations Development Account projects. The mandate derives from the proposals put forward in the 1997 report of the Secretary-General entitled “Renewing the United Nations: a programme for reform” (A/51/950); in implementing the proposals, the General Assembly, in its resolution 52/220, noted the creation of the new Department of Economic and Social Affairs.

9.2	The Department forms the backbone of the normative and analytical support provided by the Secretariat to intergovernmental bodies and processes addressing the economic, social and environmental dimensions of sustainable development.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

9.3	The world continues to suffer setbacks in development progress, characterized by heightened global challenges, including climate change, debt distress and high and rising inequalities. These are now further compounded by policy uncertainty, financial volatility, growing fragmentation and weakening international development cooperation. Countries in special situations, including the least developed countries, landlocked developing countries and small island developing States, remain particularly challenged. Middle-income countries still face challenges in achieving sustainable development.
9.4	Amid these challenges, a coherent and integrated approach in support of Member States is critical to accelerate progress in achieving the Goals.

9.5	The Department continues to champion synergistic, future-focused approaches to sustainable development, addressing multiple, interlinked challenges to improve the lives and livelihoods of all people. This includes its work on climate action, sustainable infrastructure investment and risk-informed development cooperation. The Department continues to adapt its work to address the rapidly changing global policy landscape.

9.6	The Department deploys foresight to anticipate economic, social and population trends. It generates, analyses and compiles a wide range of data and statistics to inform Member States and other stakeholders as they take stock of policy options to address global trends.
9.7	The Department identifies emerging trends and policy options for eradicating poverty, addressing climate change and inequalities and promoting opportunities for global progress.

9.8	In 2027, the Department will intensify its intergovernmental, analytical and capacity-development efforts to support Member States for the 2027 Sustainable Development Goals Summit, focusing on accelerating progress towards the Goals in a rapidly changing global policy environment shaped by rising uncertainty and fragmentation. Core areas of delivery include the implementation of the Sevilla Commitment, with an emphasis on mobilizing development financing, reforming the international financial architecture and improving international tax cooperation; the follow-up to the Doha Political Declaration of the “World Social Summit” and the Pact for the Future; support for the General Assembly review of the Economic and Social Council and the high-level political forum on sustainable development processes; and the strengthening of data systems and evidence for monitoring the Goals through capacity development. The Department will continue to provide strategic substantive support to the resident coordinator system and United Nations country teams, enhancing national implementation of internationally agreed objectives, with a focus on countries in Africa and countries in special situations. It will also support development cooperation with middle-income countries, in accordance with its mandate.

9.9	With regard to cooperation with other entities at the global, regional, national and local levels, the Department contributes to building multi-stakeholder partnerships and capacity development in thematic areas, including forests, natural resources, water, energy, climate, the ocean, transport, science and technology, and special concerns of small island developing States.

9.10	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, the Department continues to support the quadrennial comprehensive policy review of operational activities for development of the United Nations system, in collaboration with United Nations system entities and the United Nations Sustainable Development Group. In collaboration with the Development Coordination Office, the Department facilitates structured engagement with resident coordinators strengthening delivery of capacity-building activities. These efforts help to translate global norms and policy frameworks into national action towards the 2030 Agenda and other internationally agreed objectives. Moreover, the Department, through the expanded Executive Committee on Economic and Social Affairs Plus, promotes common approaches and synergies that support the implementation of the 2030 Agenda, including through the voluntary national reviews and reporting on progress towards achieving the Sustainable Development Goals. Furthermore, the Department continues to work with various agencies and stakeholders to improve policy analysis and knowledge products for Member States. To ensure coherence within and across topic areas, the Department continues to chair or co-chair inter-agency mechanisms for persons with disabilities, Indigenous Peoples, older persons and young people, legal identity, financing for development, statistics, and science, technology and innovation, among others. Moreover, the United Nations Economists’ Network continues to advance policy research on key current and emerging economic and financial issues, and their impact on sustainable development.
9.11	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:
(a)	Continued cooperation with policymakers and relevant national, regional and international stakeholders and entities on sustainable development issues in the various intergovernmental processes;
(b)	Continued availability of up-to-date and accurate information, data and trends for analytical products;
(c)	Recognition of the need for mitigation measures to address risks posed by growing policy uncertainty and fragmentation.

9.12	The Department integrates a gender perspective into its normative and analytical work and its support for intergovernmental bodies, including in its programme activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate. It continues to apply gender analysis in its work and highlight issues related to gender equality in the substantive documentation and deliberations of intergovernmental bodies, as appropriate.

9.13	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, the Department promotes inclusive sustainable development for all, including persons with disabilities, and realizes the promise of the 2030 Agenda to leave no one behind. As secretariat to the Conference of States Parties to the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities, the Department facilitates global dialogue to address existing gaps in translating international commitments to persons with disabilities into development policies, programming and practices at the national and international levels. This includes the compilation and analysis of disability data and the highlighting of best practices for fostering more accessible, disability-inclusive societies.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

9.14	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative. In particular, the tables of deliverables reflect the updated planned deliverables for 2026, in line with the approved programme budget for 2026. In addition, the Department is proposing a realignment of workstreams and responsibilities across subprogrammes to further advance sustainable, inclusive and science- and evidence-based solutions for the 2030 Agenda and better reflect emerging policy trends and practical strategies for achieving the Goals. In particular, an expansion of the scope of subprogramme 7 to include the science, technology and innovation clusters is proposed. This includes the coordination of the Technology Facilitation Mechanism and the multi-stakeholder forum on science, technology and innovation for the Sustainable Development Goals, as well as the online platform on existing science, technology and innovation initiatives (2030 Connect), which were previously reflected under subprogramme 3. This adjustment reflects the growing importance of digital transformation, emerging technologies and data-driven governance, which are increasingly shaping how institutions design policies and deliver services. Moreover, it will further strengthen cross-regional learning and help to ensure that United Nations support for institutions and digital transformation remains coherent, innovative and aligned with intergovernmental frameworks. It will also enable the Department to provide more integrated support to Member States to elevate global policy discussions, strengthen multi-stakeholder engagement and help institutions to equip learners with the knowledge and skills needed to build a more sustainable future. This includes enhancing synergies in capacity-building in the interrelated areas of science, technology and digital transformation. In line with these adjustments, subprogramme 7 has been renamed “Institutions and digital transformation”, and the applicable planned activities and deliverables are reflected under subprogramme 7.

9.15	The Department will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practices of the competent intergovernmental organs. This includes, among others, any decisions concerning proposals for strengthening collaboration with the United Nations Conference on Trade and Development (UNCTAD), the regional commissions, the Office of the High Representative for the Least Developed Countries, Landlocked Developing Countries and Small Island Developing States, and other entities through joint mechanisms. The Department will explore joint options for strengthening macroeconomic analysis, with a view to contributing more harmonized analysis and policy support to Member States and building a United Nations system data commons to better link data, statistics and insights. The Department will also consider proposals aimed at streamlining support for the least developed countries, landlocked developing countries and small island developing States and strengthening and optimizing organizational arrangements in this regard, as appropriate. Furthermore, the Department will explore, in collaboration with UNCTAD, the establishment of joint knowledge hubs, co-led by the best-placed entities and linked to regional and resident coordinator offices, to pool knowledge on key issues spread across United Nations system entities.

9.18	The following evaluations completed in 2025 have guided the proposed programme plan for 2027:
(a)	Office of Internal Oversight Services (OIOS) thematic evaluation: SDG climate-focused partnerships;
(b)	System-wide evaluation: United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy;
(c)	System-wide evaluation: Progress towards a “new generation of United Nations country teams”.

9.19	In response to the results of the evaluations referenced above, the Department is, inter alia: (a) strengthening the use of partnerships to advance Sustainable Development Goals related to addressing the adverse impacts of climate change; (b) engaging in Secretariat-wide partnership platforms to address common partnership challenges; (c) strengthening standards for disability inclusion and accountability; (d) ensuring more effective and efficient implementation of the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy; and (e) streamlining the monitoring frameworks of the quadrennial comprehensive policy review and the resident coordinator system to ensure that they are more strategic and results-oriented.
9.20	The following evaluations are planned for 2027:
(a)	OIOS programme evaluation on the Department’s subprogrammes;
(b)	Internal and/or external evaluations on cross-cutting or thematic topics across subprogrammes, subject to the availability of resources;
(c)	Evaluations on capacity-building activities.
Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – Intergovernmental support and coordination for sustainable development
Objective

9.21	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance the roles of the General Assembly, the Economic and Social Council and the high-level political forum on sustainable development in the integrated implementation of and follow-up to the 2030 Agenda and the outcomes of other United Nations conferences and summits in the economic, social, environmental and related fields.
Strategy

9.22	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will continue to support the Economic and Social Council and the high-level political forum on sustainable development in the promotion and review of the integrated implementation of the 2030 Agenda and related international commitments within the context of the follow-up to the 2023 political declaration adopted at the high-level political forum convened under the auspices of the General Assembly and the ministerial declaration of the high-level segment of the 2025 session of the Economic and Social Council and the 2025 high-level political forum on sustainable development. Specifically, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Provide evidence-based analysis and advice to the Council, including to its President and Bureau, by: (i) facilitating policy dialogue and the formulation of policy recommendations, supporting the follow-up of major global sustainable development-related commitments; (ii) monitoring and leveraging the work of its subsidiary bodies and United Nations system entities; (iii) identifying new and emerging issues impacting the Sustainable Development Goals; (iv) reinforcing the links between policy discussions and reviews and national sustainable development efforts; and (v) supporting the implementation of the outcome of the General Assembly review of the Economic and Social Council and the high-level political forum at its eightieth session;

(b)	Support the high-level political forum on sustainable development held under the auspices of the Council and the General Assembly (the 2027 Sustainable Development Goals Summit) on the follow-up to and implementation of the 2030 Agenda by: (i) facilitating the progress review, policy dialogue and development of policy recommendations for the accelerated achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals and the 2030 Agenda, including through enhanced advocacy and communication efforts; (ii) assisting countries in preparing evidence-based, inclusive and analytical voluntary national reviews of their implementation of the 2030 Agenda; and (iii) engaging United Nations country teams, other subprogrammes of the Department and United Nations system entities in supporting the review process;
(c)	Provide substantive support, upon request, to policy discussions and negotiations of the General Assembly, notably its Second Committee (economic and financial issues);

(d)	Provide evidence and analysis for Member States to review and guide the operational activities of the United Nations development system based on mandates from the General Assembly’s 2024 quadrennial comprehensive policy review of the operational activities for development of the United Nations system, including through the redesigned quadrennial comprehensive policy review monitoring framework, in collaboration with United Nations system entities;

(e)	Support the resident coordinator system in guiding United Nations country teams in assisting countries in conducting voluntary national reviews and in enabling the country teams to provide coordinated assessment of the implementation of the quadrennial comprehensive policy review, in coordination with the Development Coordination Office;

(f)	Support the work of the Ad Hoc Advisory Group on Haiti in providing advice on the long-term development strategy of the country, promoting socioeconomic recovery, reconstruction and sustainability and encouraging international support, based on Haiti’s national development priorities;

(g)	Provide analysis, evidence and recommendations on effective, people-centred and gender-sensitive approaches to address multiple and interlinked global development challenges and accelerate the implementation of the 2030 Agenda, including addressing the impacts of those challenges on women and people in vulnerable situations, ensuring that no one is left behind;

(h)	Support the engagement of non-governmental organizations (NGOs) in the work of the Council and the participation of major groups and other stakeholders in relevant intergovernmental processes, including the high-level political forum on sustainable development;

(i)	Strengthen the engagement of young people in the work of the United Nations by improving the annual Council youth forum, in cooperation with the United Nations Youth Office and subprogramme 2, and facilitating the participation of youth speakers in other Council segments, forums, special meetings and the high-level political forum on sustainable development.
9.23	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Enhanced policy and operational guidance by the General Assembly and the Economic and Social Council on the implementation of the 2030 Agenda at the global, regional and country levels;

(b)	A strengthened Council and high-level political forum on sustainable development, which will include improved understanding of the role of the Council as a principal Charter body for the coordinated and integrated implementation of the 2030 Agenda and that of the high-level political forum for more effective follow-up and review of the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals;

(c)	Better understanding among Member States, United Nations system organizations and other stakeholders of the multiple and interlinked global challenges affecting implementation of the 2030 Agenda, and of policy options and practical measures for addressing those challenges, while accelerating progress towards the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals and the implementation of the Pact for the Future, and better coordination and coherence in the global response to ongoing challenges;

(d)	Progress at the country level towards realizing the Sustainable Development Goals, including in countries presenting voluntary national reviews at the high-level political forum on sustainable development;

(e)	Effective, efficient and coordinated United Nations development system support towards implementing the 2030 Agenda and responding to global development challenges, including support for voluntary national reviews, as well as improved and clearer guidance from Member States to the United Nations development system;
(f)	Increased and coordinated international support for Haiti’s long-term development, stability and sustainable development;
(g)	The voice of young people being heard at deliberations through the Council’s youth forum;

(h)	Improved engagement of NGOs in the work of the United Nations through support for the Committee on Non-Governmental Organizations, including through the integrated civil society organization system;
(i)	Increased participation of major groups and other stakeholders in the work of the high-level political forum on sustainable development, as mandated in General Assembly resolution 67/290.
Programme performance in 2025
Reaffirmed political commitments to the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development on its tenth anniversary

9.24	The tenth anniversary of the 2030 Agenda was a critical juncture for Member States and stakeholders, who marked the occasion with productive and meaningful dialogue, engagement in intergovernmental decision-making and contributions to renewed political commitment for the Sustainable Development Goals. The subprogramme supported the Economic and Social Council, including its various segments and forums, and the high-level political forum on sustainable development. The subprogramme organized the 2025 high-level political forum, bringing together more than 6,000 participants, including more than 800 stakeholder representatives, reviewed progress and policy options for accelerating progress on the Goals under review (Goals 3, 5, 8, 14 and 17) and provided a platform for 35 voluntary national reviews.

Result 3: strengthened Economic and Social Council guidance to the United Nations development system for improved delivery of operational activities for development – Proposed programme plan for 2027

9.30	Economic and Social Council system-wide guidance is essential to ensure that the United Nations development system delivers operational activities for development with greater efficiency, transparency and accountability. The subprogramme supports the Council’s oversight role by managing the collection, validation and analysis of system-wide performance data, enabling Member States to assess trends, identify gaps and provide evidence-based guidance to strengthen United Nations support for the 2030 Agenda.
Lessons learned and planned change

9.31	The lesson for the subprogramme was that the monitoring tools were complex and that more could be done to simplify their use by Member States. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will introduce impact-focused performance indicators, digitalize data management and analysis, and streamline surveys and other data collection instruments to strengthen evidence-based decision-making and reduce the burden on stakeholders.

Subprogramme 2 – Inclusive social development
Objective

9.34	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance policies for the eradication of poverty, the reduction in inequality and the achievement of greater social inclusion and well-being for all.
Strategy
9.35	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Conduct evidence-based research and analysis, including on Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, older persons and young people;

(b)	Monitor national and global socioeconomic trends to identify emerging issues and assess their implications for social policy at the national and international levels, which will help Member States to make progress towards achieving the commitments made in the Copenhagen Declaration on Social Development, the Doha Political Declaration of the “World Social Summit” under the title “the Second World Summit for Social Development”, the Pact for the Future, and Sustainable Development Goals 1 to 11 and 16;

(c)	Provide substantive support for intergovernmental dialogue and processes, including the sessions of the Commission for Social Development, the Permanent Forum on Indigenous Issues, the Conference of States Parties to the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities, the Economic and Social Council and the General Assembly;

(d)	Provide analyses, technical advice and capacity development to requesting countries in partnership with resident coordinators and United Nations entities. The subprogramme’s analytical work will include a focus on reducing inequality, insecurity and strengthening social protection systems, and it will advance the situation of those in vulnerable situations and engage more closely with United Nations country teams to enhance action at the national level;

(e)	Support Member States on issues related to shocks and recovery by promoting socially inclusive policy responses designed to eradicate poverty, reduce inequalities and protect the most vulnerable populations.
9.36	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Enhanced knowledge and awareness of Member States to ensure that no one is left behind;
(b)	Recognition by Member States of specific social development issues as a basis for action at the national level;

(c)	Increased capacity of Member States to effectively implement the social dimension of the 2030 Agenda and the commitments contained in the Copenhagen Declaration on Social Development and the Doha Political Declaration of the “World Social Summit” under the title “the Second World Summit for Social Development”;
(d)	Progress of Member States in achieving more resilient and inclusive societies, placing the world back on track to implementing the 2030 Agenda.
Programme performance in 2025
The World Social Report 2025 informed social policy dialogue in intergovernmental bodies

9.37	Global trends such as growing economic insecurity and inequality, declining levels of trust in public institutions and social fragmentation can negatively impact societies. The subprogramme leveraged external and United Nations system partnerships to produce the World Social Report 2025, highlighting current social challenges and underscoring the need for policies grounded in equity, economic security and solidarity. The Report was co-produced with the United Nations University World Institute for Development Economics Research and generated strong interest and uptake from Member States, senior leadership and international partners. Launched in the lead-up to the Second World Summit for Social Development, the Report was widely cited and referenced in policy discussions and deliberations.

children.text: Result 3: improved quality of life of older persons through the fifth review and appraisal of the implementation of the Madrid International Plan of Action on Ageing – Proposed programme plan for 2027

9.43	The Madrid International Plan of Action on Ageing, adopted in 2002, provides a framework of commitments and policy approaches related to population ageing and older persons. Its implementation is reviewed every five years. Participation in that review ensures that national policies and strategies are evaluated through a bottom‑up, participatory process involving older persons. By identifying policy gaps and priority actions, the review process supports more effective national responses that directly affect the well-being and quality of life of older persons. The subprogramme has supported Member States’ review of the Madrid Plan of Action through analytical work and capacity-building.
Lessons learned and planned change

9.44	The lesson for the subprogramme was the need to strengthen the support to Member States to mainstream ageing into national development strategies and to enhance awareness of the Madrid Plan of Action. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will organize technical assistance and awareness-raising projects to support inclusive national reviews of the Madrid Plan of Action.

Subprogramme 3 – Sustainable development
Objective

9.47	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to accelerate the implementation of the 2030 Agenda, including the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals and their targets and commitments, through increased engagement of Member States and other stakeholders.
Strategy
9.48	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Prepare analytical reports and assessments, maintain online databases and organize thematic expert meetings and conferences on specific Sustainable Development Goals and their interlinkages;

(b)	Organize, in the lead up to the high-level political forum on sustainable development, expert group meetings and conferences for Member States, on the themes of the forum and the Sustainable Development Goals that are under review;

(c)	Play a central role in inter-agency coordination mechanisms on water, energy, small island developing States, oceans and transport, and through its long-standing cooperation with specific agencies on issues such as climate change and partnership-building for sustainable development;

(d)	Convene special events in connection with the high-level political forum on sustainable development, including the Sustainable Development Goals business forum and the local and regional governments forum, enabling the sharing of lessons learned and good practices among Member States and stakeholders in the development arena;

(e)	Organize, in coordination with the regional commissions, resident coordinator offices and United Nations country teams, as applicable, capacity-building activities at the global, regional and national levels on the above topics.
9.49	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Increased knowledge of Member States and stakeholders of the 2030 Agenda and its Sustainable Development Goals, including for their deliberations during the high-level political forum on sustainable development and other related forums, and for the development of related national policies and programmes;

(b)	Increased integration of Sustainable Development Goals into inclusive national planning processes, policies and strategies, and increased action and initiatives taken by Member States and other stakeholders to accelerate the implementation of the 2030 Agenda.
Programme performance in 2025
Implementation plan for the United Nations Decade of Sustainable Transport (2026–2035) launched

9.50	Member States called for the preparation of an implementation plan for the United Nations Decade of Sustainable Transport (2026–2035) to catalyse action in this area. The subprogramme, in collaboration with the regional economic commissions and in consultation with Member States and other relevant stakeholders, prepared the implementation plan, providing a shared vision for the Decade with six priority areas and the means of implementation needed for transformative action by all stakeholders. The implementation plan strengthens the links between all modes of transport to achieve the Goals and emphasizes the accessibility and safety and security of transport systems, focusing on the needs of women and girls and of persons with disabilities.

Subprogramme 4 – Statistics
Objective

9.60	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to strengthen the capacity of Member States with regard to the production and use of high-quality, timely, disaggregated, open data and national statistics and geospatial information as a basis on which to inform policy and decision-making for the 2030 Agenda and for other United Nations policy agendas and to review progress at the national and international levels.
Strategy
9.61	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Develop, disseminate and support the implementation by Member States of statistical and geospatial information frameworks, standards and methods, especially those related to the Sustainable Development Goals, the Integrated Geospatial Information Framework and other new emerging policy issues;

(b)	Provide technical assistance and advice to Member States through training on the modernization of statistical information systems, the Integrated Geospatial Information Framework, the use of big data, artificial intelligence and data science, population data systems, environmental-economic accounting, quality assurance, survey instruments and citizen data, to fill data gaps and leave no one excluded from decision-making and policies that affect them;

(c)	Provide substantive support to United Nations intergovernmental bodies, in particular the Statistical Commission, the Committee of Experts on Geospatial Information Management and the United Nations Group of Experts on Geographical Names, including by preparing reports and convening sessions;

(d)	Facilitate and expand the coordination and coherence of international statistical and geospatial information programmes and activities by promoting the availability, accessibility and application of geospatial information, including the standardization of geographical names, and better integration of statistical and geospatial information;

(e)	Cooperate with the resident coordinator system and United Nations country teams in data and statistical activities and their interaction with the global statistical community, including through the Global Network of Data Officers and Statisticians; cooperate closely with other international organizations and establish partnerships with civil society organizations, academia and private sector/non-traditional data providers, including through the platform of the United Nations World Data Forum;

(f)	Collect, compile and disseminate global statistical information and data in the areas of economic, social, demographic and environmental statistics, including the Sustainable Development Goal indicators, through the use of technology and interoperability standards, such as the Statistical Data and Metadata Exchange;

(g)	Issue flagship data publications, maintain databases and data platforms and continue to modernize UNdata as a United Nations system-wide portal, and support the development of open data hubs and labs to showcase innovative uses of data, artificial intelligence and data science, and enhance the visualization of data and metadata;

(h)	Develop and share guidance and information on initiatives, tools and best practices to help national statistics systems to continue to operate and respond to the new specific data needs to address and mitigate the impact of global development challenges on societies, advance responsible, equitable and interoperable data governance approaches, address issues of open and timely access to critical data needed by Governments and all sectors of society to accelerate the implementation of the 2030 Agenda, and prepare for successor frameworks, such as those associated with “beyond gross domestic product (GDP)” and well-being measurement.
9.62	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Enhanced quality, availability and access to policy-relevant statistical and geospatial information;

(b)	Increased production and dissemination by Member States of better data and national statistics and geospatial information that are comparable and in line with the adopted updated international frameworks and standards, and provision of enhanced and open access to them;

(c)	Enhanced coordination across international organizations mandated to provide international official statistics, and improved coherence and more effective functioning of the global statistical and geospatial information systems;
(d)	Increased statistical and geospatial information capacity of Member States and the United Nations system.
Programme performance in 2025
Users have improved understanding of migration statistics

9.63	The increasing complexity of international migration presents challenges for statistical measurement and policy formulation. The Global Compact for Safe, Orderly and Regular Migration recognized the importance of data for evidence-based policies. The subprogramme coordinated the revision of the Recommendations on Statistics of International Migration. One of the key recommendations arising from that revision was focused on the implementation of effective communication strategies to improve the use and relevance of migration statistics, with an emphasis on the needs of users of such data, primarily migration policymakers. Based on the recommendations, the subprogramme strengthened its outreach to various stakeholders on issues relating to migration and migrants, fostering dialogue between users and producers of migration statistics at knowledge-sharing and capacity-building events.

Result 3: Member States implement the System of National Accounts 2025 – Proposed programme plan for 2027

9.69	The Statistical Commission adopted the System of National Accounts 2025 as an international statistical standard, following four years of concerted efforts by the global statistical community to update the System of National Accounts 2008. The System of National Accounts 2025 reflects changes in the global economy and addresses user demands for better information about well-being and sustainability. In its resolution 2025/12, the Economic and Social Council recommended that Member States use the System of National Accounts 2025 in their international reporting, while the Statistical Commission endorsed its implementation strategy, with a 2029–2030 target date. The subprogramme will continue its support to Member States that have not yet implemented the System of National Accounts 2008, which is a prerequisite for the implementation of the System of National Accounts 2025. Given that countries that have not yet implemented the System of National Accounts 2008 face more significant challenges in doing so, supporting them will require more time and greater effort. As a result, it may not be possible to achieve implementation of the System of National Accounts 2008 in more than 17 countries in the reporting period.
Lessons learned and planned change

9.70	The lesson for the subprogramme was that the broad and intensive collaboration of all stakeholders enabled the successful updating of the System of National Accounts 2008. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will use its ability to bring together all stakeholders during the implementation of the System of National Accounts 2025, ensuring that Member States have the capacity to produce national accounts in line with the updated standard.

Subprogramme 5 – Population
Objective

9.73	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to improve knowledge of global population trends, advance an evidence-based understanding of interaction between population change and sustainable development among policymakers and the public, and strengthen the international community’s capacity to effectively address current and emerging population issues and integrate the population dimension into the international development agenda.
Strategy
9.74	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Prepare studies on population growth, population ageing, migration, urbanization, fertility, family planning, mortality and other aspects of population in relation to sustainable development using population estimates and projections, the analysis of demographic trends and the evaluation of population policies;

(b)	Produce comprehensive global data sets on selected topics, as well as reports to highlight these topics and related data, and an annual report on a selected major demographic topic. Data sets will include aggregated results for groups of countries in special situations;
(c)	Provide normative guidance and capacity development on population data and analysis to requesting countries and to United Nations country teams;

(d)	Provide substantive support to United Nations intergovernmental bodies, in particular the Commission on Population and Development, including by preparing reports and convening expert meetings and panels;

(e)	Assist Governments in assessing progress made in implementing the Programme of Action of the International Conference on Population and Development held in 1994, focusing on the global monitoring of Sustainable Development Goal indicators on reproductive health, international migration, child and maternal mortality and population ageing, often in partnership with other entities of the United Nations system;

(f)	Improve the United Nations estimates and projections of the global population and study the interaction of COVID-19 and other global development challenges with demographic phenomena, including mortality, population ageing, fertility and family planning and international migration, and including these analyses, as appropriate, in publications and technical materials.
9.75	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Improved access to data and reports on population matters for Governments and other users and increased use of the subprogramme’s data and findings in the work of stakeholders;

(b)	Increased awareness of population trends and their relationship to sustainable development through accurate data and policy-relevant reports, benefiting Member States and other stakeholders in policy and development planning;
(c)	Advancement in the international community’s use of scientifically grounded information about the demographic impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic and other global development challenges.
Programme performance in 2025
National and district population projections produced using internationally recognized probabilistic methods

9.76	Accurate subnational demographic data are essential for equitable resource allocation and monitoring of the Sustainable Development Goals. Following the 2024 Population and Housing Census, Uganda requested robust projections to guide its national development plan, implementation of Agenda 2063 of the African Union and implementation of the 2030 Agenda. The subprogramme conducted a technical workshop and advisory mission in June 2025, providing training to 18 national experts in advanced demographic methods. Following international best practices, the training covered the most recent protocols for census evaluation and adjustment, Bayesian probabilistic methods for fertility, mortality and migration, and extensions for subnational applications.

9.82	Robust estimates of international migration and country capacity to collect data on policy-relevant indicators are critical to monitoring the migration-related targets of the 2030 Agenda and the implementation of the Global Compact for Safe, Orderly and Regular Migration. The subprogramme has released methodologically upgraded estimates of the number of international migrants and co-led the development of a proposed limited set of indicators to support voluntary national reviews of Global Compact progress.
Lessons learned and planned change

9.83	The lesson for the subprogramme was that further efforts were needed to apply the new estimation methods for all countries and areas, improve disaggregation of estimates and provide support to countries to report on migration-related Sustainable Development Goals and the implementation of the Global Compact. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will release updated estimates disaggregated by age, sex, origin and destination, update Goal indicator 10.7.2 and expand capacity-building initiatives.

Subprogramme 6 – Economic analysis and policy
Objective

9.86	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to strengthen national capacities and international policy coordination for identifying and managing short-term macroeconomic risks, to shape medium- and long-term socioeconomic policies for development and to accelerate sustainable development, especially of least developed countries, small island developing States and other countries in special situations.
Strategy
9.87	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	At the national, regional and global levels, monitor and forecast the macroeconomic situation, focusing on countries in special situations, including the least developed countries, and support Member States in the formulation of global, regional and national policies through expert group meetings, country-level empirical evidence analysis and background studies on short- and long-term economic development thematic issues;

(b)	Broaden the scope of economic forecasting and simulation tools to assess policy trade-offs in promoting economic growth, while reducing poverty and inequality and enhancing environmental sustainability, in line with the 2030 Agenda;

(c)	Support the Committee for Development Policy and provide analytical input to the intergovernmental processes related to the least developed countries, as well as to the annual themes of meetings of the Economic and Social Council and of the high-level political forum on sustainable development;

(d)	Design and implement capacity development activities for a range of countries, including for graduating and recently graduated least developed countries, enhancing capacity for economic planning, monitoring and policymaking;

(e)	Examine rapid technological change, noting both its constructive and disruptive potential and identifying the strategies and policies necessary to harness its positive potential for achieving sustainable development, while mitigating its possible negative effects;
(f)	Provide policy guidance and recommendations on selected topics to Member States for recovery from recurrent shocks and challenges, building on the subprogramme’s analytical work;

(g)	Provide analytical input and customized online resources to the resident coordinator system for supporting Member States and for the preparation of common country analyses and United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Frameworks, and other pertinent topics;

(h)	Deliver the substantive leadership and coordination of the United Nations Economists’ Network, bringing together United Nations system economists to share perspectives from the local, national, regional and global levels;

(i)	Maintain the global economic outlook database, the least developed country criteria database, the Support Measures Portal for Least Developed Countries on international support measures and other online resources available to policymakers and other national and international stakeholders;

(j)	Advance the formulation of a specific response plan, aimed at better addressing the multidimensional nature of sustainable development and facilitating sustainable development cooperation and coordinated and inclusive support to middle-income countries based on their specific challenges and diverse needs.
9.88	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Greater articulation of short-term macroeconomic stability with long-term development goals, while advancing the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals, through the improved integration of economic, social and environmental priorities into national development plans and strategies;

(b)	Improved awareness among Member States of the multilateral efforts and economic policy options necessary for a sustainable recovery from recurrent shocks and challenges, consistent with the 2030 Agenda;
(c)	Improved information basis for the Committee for Development Policy and the Economic and Social Council when assessing and recommending least developed countries for graduation;

(d)	Enhanced awareness and knowledge of Member States and the international community of the development trajectory of the least developed countries, their risks and challenges and the specific support options;

(e)	Increased capacity of Member States to effectively use international support measures, prepare for graduation from the least developed country category and ensure the stability of their development progress and the realization of the 2030 Agenda;

(f)	Enhanced collaboration, coordination and efficacy of the United Nations development system, which will provide a more coherent cooperation framework towards the implementation of the 2030 Agenda and bolster sustainable development at the country level.
Programme performance in 2025
Improved digital tools to strengthen government officials’ knowledge of macroeconomics and trade

9.89	Digital tools are increasingly vital for strengthening policymaking and trade promotion, as they enable Member States to access timely information, conduct integrated assessments and build capacity for sustainable development. In 2025, the provision of support through digital tools, complementing in-person training and capacity-building, became essential as a cost‑effective solution amid unexpected resource constraints at the United Nations and heightened uncertainty in the global trading system. The subprogramme assisted Member States by developing online courses on the overlapping generations model and the climate, land, energy and water systems model, enhancing their ability to analyse complex interlinkages across macroeconomic, microeconomic and resource management frameworks. In addition, the subprogramme facilitated the use of the upgraded mobile application‑based ePing alert system, which provides Governments and stakeholders with real‑time notifications on trade‑related measures. These efforts contributed to more coherent policymaking, greater transparency and stronger engagement in international trade processes.

children.text: Result 3: least developed countries are prepared to navigate the graduation process and post-graduation landscape – Proposed programme plan for 2027

9.95	Sustainable graduation from the least developed countries category is essential to ensure that development gains are preserved and that countries continue to maintain the stability of their development progress after graduation. The Committee for Development Policy triennial review process, which identifies graduation eligibility, and the Committee’s enhanced monitoring mechanism for graduating and graduated countries provide a coherent and long-term framework for Member States to ensure sustainable graduation. The subprogramme has strengthened Member States’ knowledge on this topic and their policymaking capacity by improving data accuracy, enhancing economic analysis and fostering deeper engagement with the countries concerned.
Lessons learned and planned change

9.96	The lesson for the subprogramme was that greater emphasis is needed on early engagement with potentially graduating countries and on tailoring analytical support to their specific development contexts and economic structures. This requires complex analysis of development data and processes, as well as meaningful dialogue with national stakeholders to ensure ownership and effective follow‑up. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will continue to customize its approach to forecasting and its policy recommendations on structural transformation to the needs of graduating countries and will continue to support those countries in developing the skills to manage and analyse their own data for future analysis post-graduation.

Subprogramme 7 – Institutions and digital transformation
Objective

9.99	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance effective, accountable and inclusive governance and institutions for sustainable development and to accelerate digital transformation.
Strategy
9.100	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Service the Committee of Experts on Public Administration, including by organizing expert meetings; preparing analytical papers and technical guidelines to operationalize the principles of effective governance for sustainable development, developed by the Committee and endorsed by the Economic and Social Council in 2018, together with all relevant stakeholders; and providing input on issues related to governance and public administration to the meetings of the Council and the high-level political forum on sustainable development;
(b)	Play a central role in inter-agency coordination mechanisms in the area of science, technology and innovation;

(c)	Promote policy guidance on innovation, digital government and digital transformation, including the use of artificial intelligence, for the Sustainable Development Goals through the United Nations E-Government Survey, monitor global developments on institutional dimensions covered by Goals 16 and 17 and review national institutional arrangements for the achievement of the Goals, with a specific focus on countries in special situations, and in direct support of the follow-up on and review of the 2030 Agenda;

(d)	Foster policy dialogue and advocacy for the implementation of the outcomes of the World Summit on the Information Society and facilitate the implementation of and follow-up on its assigned action lines, namely, C1 (the role of Governments and all stakeholders in the promotion of information and communications technology (ICT) for development), C7 (ICT applications: benefits in all aspects of life (e-government)) and C11 (international and regional cooperation);

(e)	Facilitate multi-stakeholder policy dialogue on Internet governance, including discussion of public policy issues related to key elements of Internet governance, such as interoperable data governance approaches, digital inclusion and data protection (General Assembly resolution 70/125, para. 63), and shape policy decisions at the global, national and local levels by supporting the annual meeting of the Internet Governance Forum and intersessional activities;

(f)	Coordinate the work of the Technology Facilitation Mechanism, including the multi-stakeholder forum on science, technology and innovation for the Sustainable Development Goals; the United Nations Group of 10 High-level Representatives of Civil Society, the Private Sector and the Scientific Community to Promote Science, Technology and Innovation for the Sustainable Development Goals; and the online platform on existing science, technology and innovation initiatives (2030 Connect);

(g)	Expand capacity development to respond to Member States’ requests for technical cooperation on strengthening their governance, public institutions and human resources capacities in alignment with the decade of action and delivery for sustainable development, including through partnerships with regional commissions and the resident coordinator system and increased efforts to support international peer exchanges on effective governance for sustainable development;

(h)	Expand work on equipping the public sector workforce with the capacities to realize the 2030 Agenda and the Pact for the Future, which includes developing and piloting new methodologies on leadership and changing mindsets, institutional arrangements for policy coherence, transparency and integrity in the public service, strategic foresight, digital government and interoperable data governance approaches, and innovation for public service delivery, with an emphasis on countries in special situations, in collaboration with the resident coordinator system;

(i)	Support Member States in promoting innovation in public governance through follow-up mechanisms to the United Nations Public Service Day Awards Ceremony and Forum, such as peer-to-peer learning for the transfer and adaptation of innovative practices, including on gender-sensitive analysis, and through the United Nations Public Administration Network;

(j)	Support supreme audit institutions on auditing Sustainable Development Goals achievement by providing analytical work, expert advice, technical assistance and capacity-building, on the basis of existing demand for support by groups of the International Organization of Supreme Audit Institutions;
(k)	Promote and leverage public governance innovative practices and digital government strategies action plans and road maps.
9.101	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Enhanced awareness and knowledge of emerging governance and public administration issues at the national and subnational levels and countries contextualizing institutional aspects of their voluntary national reviews so that Member States can develop their capacities to promote effective governance for sustainable development in a dynamic international context;
(b)	Increased capacities of Member States to build effective, accountable and inclusive institutions at all levels to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals and deliver on the Pact for the Future;

(c)	Increased capacity of Member States in designing and implementing effective and inclusive digital government policies, strategies and services to improve people’s lives at all levels of government;

(d)	Improved capacities of public servants to deliver services to people in areas such as health and education, justice, social protection and employment, focusing on professionalization of the public sector workforce, an ethical, responsive and ICT-enabled public administration and progress towards gender equality in the public sector;
(e)	Increased use of science and technology to leverage their full potential for the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals by Member States and other stakeholders;
(f)	Adjustments by Member States at the structural, organizational and programme levels to accelerate Sustainable Development Goals delivery;
(g)	Adoption of digital government strategies to build resilient societies and close digital divides.
Programme performance in 2025
Supreme audit institutions assessed the implementation of Sustainable Development Goal 13 on climate action

9.102	Supreme audit institutions are uniquely positioned to strengthen climate action under Sustainable Development Goal 13 through independent audits and assessments. The subprogramme provided technical assistance to 18 supreme audit institutions, led by the Federal Court of Accounts of Brazil, to develop a standardized methodology and web-based tool, ClimateScanner, for assessing government actions and progress in addressing adverse impacts of climate change. This included peer learning among supreme audit institutions, experience-sharing on auditing climate action and discussions of the roll-out of these assessments, which involved auditors from several countries, including least developed countries and small island developing States. The assessments identified constraints, key risks and priority areas in governance, finance and public policy related to climate change. The findings are aimed at helping Governments to improve climate action and at accelerating the implementation of Goal 13 at the national level. The results were presented at the Conference of the Parties to the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change in 2024 and 2025.

children.text: Result 3: public officials apply behavioural insights and adopt good practices to advance innovation and digital transformation – Proposed programme plan for 2027

9.108	Rapid technological, social and institutional changes continue to shape the demands placed on public institutions, which underscores the need for more innovative technological tools to improve public services. The subprogramme has prepared analytical products and conducted capacity development activities to enable public officials to accelerate innovation and use digital tools to modernize public services.
Lessons learned and planned change

9.109	The lesson for the subprogramme was that efforts to introduce innovation and digital tools in public institutions lead to lasting results only when they are paired with intentional changes in how the institutions operate, including change in organizational culture. Reforms are also effective when guidance is tailored to each setting and considers both technical solutions and organizational practices. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will place greater emphasis on understanding how people and organizations actually behave and expand customized tools and peer-learning opportunities to better respond to the changing needs of Member States.

Subprogramme 8 – Sustainable forest management
Objective

9.112	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure the effective implementation of sustainable management of all types of forests at all levels in accordance with the United Nations Strategic Plan for Forests 2017–2030.
Strategy
9.113	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Support the intergovernmental forest policy dialogue through the United Nations Forum on Forests, in accordance with the strategic plan, the quadrennial programme of work (2025–2028) and the outcomes of the nineteenth session of the Forum, including the 2024 midterm review of the effectiveness of the International Arrangement on Forests, and coordinate the Forum’s input to the high-level political forum on sustainable development;
(b)	Advance coordination and cooperation with relevant United Nations entities, member organizations of the Collaborative Partnership on Forests and other stakeholders;

(c)	Develop the online clearinghouse of the Global Forest Financing Facilitation Network and assist countries and partners in designing forest financing strategies, facilitating access to financing and assisting Member States in mobilizing resources for sustainable forest management;

(d)	Place an emphasis on the special needs and circumstances of Africa, the least developed countries, countries with low forest cover, countries with high forest cover, countries with medium forest cover and low deforestation and small island developing States, as well as countries with economies in transition (Economic and Social Council resolution 2017/4, para. 63);

(e)	Advance the implementation of the United Nations Strategic Plan for Forests 2017–2030, including through assisting countries in developing a robust national reporting format for the submission of national reports to the United Nations Forum on Forests, preparing the second Global Forest Goals Report and implementation of the strategic plan’s communication and outreach strategy;

(f)	Support common country analyses, which inform the United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Framework, thereby providing resident coordinators with the tools to tailor their responses to countries’ specific needs and priorities and to support the implementation of the 2030 Agenda, including through national implementation of sustainable forest management;

(g)	Support Member States, as appropriate, on issues related to sustainable forest management, through its normative work, technical assistance and capacity-building activities, including the international policy dialogue on the integration of sustainable forest management into decision-making processes concerning land use planning and development, in line with United Nations Forum on Forests resolutions.
9.114	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Strengthened political commitment and action for sustainable forest management, leading to increased contributions by forests to Sustainable Development Goals achievement, including on climate change, biodiversity, land and greater integration of forests into national sustainable development plans and poverty reduction strategies;

(b)	Improved capacity of eligible countries to mobilize and gain access to financing for sustainable forest management, and improved information on and access to existing and emerging multilateral and other funding sources and data;
(c)	Member States’ progress towards achieving the Global Forest Goals and the forest-related Sustainable Development Goals;

(d)	Increased awareness among Member States of the benefits of forests based on the 2027 theme of the International Day of Forests, and on highlighting the interdependent role of forests in enabling the world to meet the Sustainable Development Goals that will be reviewed during the 2027 high-level political forum on sustainable development.
Programme performance in 2025
Member States commence implementation of the new quadrennial programme of work (2025–2028) to accelerate the United Nations Strategic Plan for Forests

9.115	The adoption of the quadrennial programme of work for the period 2025–2028 is critical to the implementation of the United Nations Strategic Plan for Forests 2017–2030 because it will ensure that global forest policy aligns with the Sustainable Development Goals. The subprogramme provided technical assistance and specialized expertise to Member States, identifying priority areas regarding issues of global concern for forests. These contributions facilitated Member States’ deliberations, enabling the alignment of national initiatives and resources with the priorities for the period 2025–2028.

children.text: Result 3: strengthened commitment and action by the International Arrangement on Forests to achieve the Global Forest Goals – Proposed programme plan for 2027

9.121	The United Nations Forum on Forests, the central pillar of the International Arrangement on Forests, oversees implementation of the United Nations Strategic Plan for Forests 2017–2030. The subprogramme supported Member States in reviewing and assessing their progress in implementation of the Plan. A key element of this assessment is the voluntary national reports submitted by Member States through an online reporting platform.
Lessons learned and planned change

9.122	The lesson for the subprogramme was that despite the launch of the online reporting platform in early 2025, few voluntary national reports have been submitted due to national capacity constraints on collecting forest-related data. Given that these submissions inform the Global Forest Goals Report, it is important that many more Member States provide information on their country-specific progress. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will provide training to Member States on collecting forest-related data from their national statistical systems and entering such data into the platform and facilitate platform use, ensuring a more robust data set for the final review in 2030.

Subprogramme 9 – Financing for sustainable development
Objective

9.125	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance the follow-up on and review of the implementation of the outcomes of the International Conferences on Financing for Development, including the Sevilla Commitment, and the delivery of the means of implementation of the 2030 Agenda.
Strategy
9.126	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Promote and support the follow-up to the agreements and commitments reached at the Fourth International Conference on Financing for Development;

(b)	Build on continuing analytical work in collaboration with the Inter-Agency Task Force on Financing for Development and prepare the annual Financing for Sustainable Development Report, and other reports for the Economic and Social Council forum on financing for development follow-up and for Second Committee and other deliberations;

(c)	Support reform to the international financial architecture, including the sovereign debt and development cooperation architecture, and support the development of principles of responsible borrowing and lending, inclusive dialogue on sovereign debt solutions and credit ratings, and capacity development;

(d)	Support the revitalized Development Cooperation Forum in its review of trends, progress and emerging issues in international development cooperation, with a view to promoting global knowledge-sharing and learning, enhancing the coherence, effectiveness, accountability and impact of development cooperation and providing policy guidance and recommendations; and support countries in designing and implementing integrated national financing frameworks through the Integrated National Financing Framework Facility;

(e)	Scale up investment for the Sustainable Development Goals with concrete solutions, building on the work of the Global Investors for Sustainable Development Alliance, including through the Sustainable Development Goals Investment Fair, enabling engagement between Member States and investors, along with capacity development for developing investable projects and country platforms and for improving infrastructure asset management;

(f)	Support Member States in norm-setting for international tax cooperation and in improving domestic resource mobilization to enhance progress towards the Sustainable Development Goals, thereby enhancing tax policies and administration and addressing tax evasion, aggressive tax avoidance and illicit financial flows; provide policy support to expert bodies and intergovernmental processes, including the Intergovernmental Negotiating Committee on the United Nations Framework Convention on International Tax Cooperation, along with capacity-building to foster more inclusive and effective international tax cooperation.
9.127	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Policy and action on financing for sustainable development through a strengthened analytical foundation and inclusive dialogue, including in the area of sovereign debt, for the outcomes of the Economic and Social Council forum on financing for development follow-up and Second Committee deliberations;

(b)	Enhanced coherence, effectiveness and accountability of international development cooperation through the use, by Governments and stakeholders, of policy guidance and recommendations for the effective implementation of the 2030 Agenda, the Sevilla Commitment, the Paris Agreement and the Sendai Framework; countries designing and using integrated national financing frameworks, including actionable policy options, to mobilize financing for sustainable development;
(c)	Accelerated mobilization of resources for the Sustainable Development Goals, leading to greater investment opportunities with positive impacts, including in countries in special situations;

(d)	Improved solutions at all levels for strengthening international tax cooperation, including through the United Nations framework convention on international tax cooperation and its two protocols, improving domestic resource mobilization and more effective implementation by Governments and other stakeholders of United Nations norms, policy and practical guidance on tax matters.
Programme performance in 2025
A new financing framework, the Sevilla Commitment, adopted as a follow-up to the Addis Ababa Action Agenda

9.128	At the Fourth International Conference on Financing for Development, the Sevilla Commitment was adopted as a follow-up to the Addis Ababa Action Agenda, with a view to strengthening international cooperation to mobilize financing for sustainable development and accelerate progress towards the Sustainable Development Goals. The subprogramme supported Member States in advancing the intergovernmental process and provided substantive and organizational support to the Conference and the sessions of its preparatory committee, including analytical inputs and logistical, administrative and substantive services. These efforts facilitated inclusive and substantive engagement among Member States on the Sevilla Commitment. Moreover, Member States agreed on policy commitments and follow-up mechanisms that were reflected in the Sevilla Commitment, including new high-level meetings on financial integrity and credit ratings.

children.text: Planned results for 2027

Result 1: strengthened engagement of Member States in the financing for development follow-up process to improve coherence and implementation of agreed outcomes – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

9.130	The subprogramme’s work contributed to 187 countries participating in the intergovernmental follow-up process to the international conferences on financing for development, which exceeded the planned target of 120 countries. Given that the increase in the number of countries participating in the intergovernmental follow-up process in 2025 was specifically due to the Fourth International Conference on Financing for Development, the planned target for 2027 is lower than the actual number of countries participating in 2025 because the other intergovernmental follow-up processes that have a similar scale and attendance are expected to take place after 2027.

Result 2: enhanced engagement of developing countries in shaping international tax norms – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

9.132	The subprogramme’s work contributed to 56 developing countries engaging in shaping international tax norms in the work of the Intergovernmental Negotiating Committee on the United Nations Framework Convention on International Tax Cooperation, which exceeded the planned target of 45 countries.
9.133	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 9.X).
Figure 9.X
Performance measure: number of developing countries engaging in shaping international tax norms (cumulative)
Result 3: international debt architecture is operationalized – Proposed programme plan for 2027

9.134	Debt challenges continue to be a constraint on sustainable development. In 2025, Member States adopted an ambitious package of debt actions in the Sevilla Commitment, complemented by debt initiatives set out in the Sevilla Platform for Action. The subprogramme supported this agenda, coordinating a system-wide follow-up, including through a working group on responsible borrowing and lending principles, the United Nations dialogue on debt and the Sevilla Forum on Debt.
Lessons learned and planned change

9.135	The lesson for the subprogramme was that progress on debt requires sustained engagement with relevant stakeholders through analytical, convening and capacity-building functions. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will strengthen the link between these functions. It will provide support to the working group and the intergovernmental dialogues on debt and support debt initiatives under the Sevilla Platform for Action.
